Short answer: yes — a few simple technique changes will make a big difference. Based on what you’ve been doing (beef stew, wanting more plant-based and slow-cooker yogurt recipes), here are focused, practical tips you’ll actually use:

1. Use a thermometer (especially for yogurt). You already noted this helps a lot — temp control is the single biggest factor in consistent yogurt and in avoiding over- or under-cooked dishes.  
2. Brown first. Sear meat or sauté aromatics (onion, garlic, spices) in a pan before adding to the slow cooker for deeper flavor. This is one of the fastest flavor upgrades.  
3. Mind fill level and liquid. Don’t overfill — ~2/3 is a good rule — and remember slow cookers lose little liquid, so reduce added broth slightly from stovetop recipes. If a sauce is too thin, thicken at the end (cornstarch slurry, reduce on high, or finish in a pan).  
4. Cut evenly and plan timing. Cut vegetables to uniform sizes; add quick-cooking veg (peas, zucchini, delicate greens) toward the end so they don’t turn to mush. Root veg and dense items can go in at the start.  
5. Salt late (but not always). Salting too early can concentrate/over-salt some recipes; for stews, season gradually and finish-taste at the end.  
6. Layer for best texture. Put denser items that need more heat nearest the cooker’s heat source (often the bottom).  
7. Herb timing for better flavor. Use dried herbs early in the cook, fresh herbs and delicate aromatics at the end to preserve brightness.  
8. For vegan/cashew yogurt specifically: start with an unsweetened, unflavored vegan starter (or plain yogurt with live cultures); when making cashew milk, strain with cheesecloth or a nut-milk bag if you want ultra-smooth texture. Incubation/control of heat is key (use your thermometer and don’t disturb the lid while it cultures). After it’s made you can flavor/sweeten to taste.  
9. Small tweaks for plant-based slow-cooker meals: boost umami with miso/soy/tomato paste, add beans later if you want them firmer, and pre-soak beans if using dried (unless using a tested slow-cooker bean recipe).

   - Download playlists for offline use rather than streaming. Lower streaming quality when necessary.

9. Notifications & sync frequency
   - Reduce push email frequency, disable unnecessary notifications and auto‑sync for noncritical accounts.

   - Avoid deep 0%→100% cycles regularly; try to keep the battery between ~20–80% when convenient. Avoid charging in direct sun or hot cars (beach/travel). Remove case if phone gets hot while charging.

rows.ground_truth: The user would prefer responses that highlight the differences between Fender Stratocaster and Gibson Les Paul electric guitars, such as the feel of the neck, weight, and sound profile. They might not prefer general tips on buying an electric guitar or suggestions that do not take into account their current guitar and desired upgrade.
rows.predicted_answer:
Nice — since you’ve been thinking about moving from a Fender Stratocaster toward a Gibson Les Paul and even experimenting with open D, here are focused things to look for at the music store (and what you’ll probably prefer vs. avoid).

Quick preference summary
- You’ll likely prefer a Les Paul–style electric if you want warm, full tone, lots of sustain, and thicker neck feels.
- You’ll probably not prefer a Strat’s brighter, thinner single‑coil sound if you’re chasing the Les Paul vibe — but it’s worth comparing both to be sure.

Shopping/test checklist
- Playability
  - Neck profile and feel: try several neck shapes/thumb positions until one feels comfortable for your hands.
  - Action & fretwork: check for buzzing when fretting across the neck and try bending strings—smooth frets and low buzz are musts.
  - Scale length feel: compare a Les Paul (shorter 24.75") vs a Strat (25.5") if you care about string tension and bending ease.
- Tone and electronics
  - Pickup types: humbuckers (Les Paul) = thicker, quieter, more sustain; single‑coils (Strat) = brighter, spankier. Try neck/middle/bridge positions.
  - Try clean and driven amp settings to hear full sonic range (bring notes you play, or test both a clean Fender‑style amp and a cranked amp).
  - Test pickup switching and volume/tone pots for scratch/noise.
- Sustain & resonance
  - Strum a note and mute the strings—Les Pauls usually ring longer. That sustain is one of the reasons you’d pick a Les Paul.
- Setup & stability
  - Check tuning stability (tuners, bridge), intonation up and down the neck, and whether the guitar holds tune in alternate tunings (try open D if that’s important to you).
  - Ask about recent setups or if a professional setup is needed — many store guitars need minor adjustments.
- Hardware & condition (for used instruments)
  - Inspect frets, nut slots, bridge, tuners, and wiring; look for cracks or repaired headstock/body damage.
  - Check strap buttons, case/gig bag included, serial number/age, and any non‑factory mods.
- Weight & ergonomics
  - Les Pauls can be noticeably heavier — make sure it’s comfortable for long playing or standing.
  - If you like the Les Paul sound but want less weight, ask about chambered/weight‑relieved models.
- Practicals
  - Bring your usual amp cable and a pick you use; use the same strings/gauge you play at home if possible.
  - Ask about return policy/warranty and whether the store will set the guitar up before you take it home.
  - If budget is a factor, compare new vs used — a well‑set used Les Paul can be a great value.

Play test routine (quick)
- Play open chords, single‑note lines, bends, and palm‑muted riffs.
- Try the positions you use most (including open D) and listen for tone, sustain, fret buzz, and tuning stability.
- Switch pickup positions and roll tone/volume knobs while playing to hear usable sounds.

If you want, tell me your budget and whether you lean more toward rock/clean/metal tones and I’ll make model suggestions that fit that profile.

- 1 tsp lemon zest (use zest, not much juice to avoid curdling)
- 1 tbsp grated fresh ginger (or 1 tsp ground ginger)
- 1/2 tsp vanilla extract
- 1–2 tsp honey (or monk fruit/stevia to taste)
Method: Warm almond milk with ginger (don’t boil) for 5 minutes to infuse, remove from heat, stir in lemon zest, vanilla and sweetener; strain if you used fresh ginger. Chill and store 4–5 days; shake before use.
